name: Apple Memory Integrity Enforcement (MIE)
summary: Apple's production deployment of hardware-assisted memory safety: Enhanced MTE in synchronous mode plus secure typed allocators (kalloc_type, xzone malloc) plus Tag Confidentiality Enforcement against speculative side channels. One of the most aggressive consumer deployments of hardware memory tagging to date.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Apple's **MIE** is one of the most aggressive consumer deployments of hardware-assisted memory safety to date, combining **Enhanced MTE (EMTE)** in synchronous mode, secure typed allocators (`kalloc_type`, `xzone malloc`), and Tag Confidentiality Enforcement against speculative side channels.

Apple pushed Arm to specify EMTE (2022) so synchronous tag-check is cheap enough for production rather than debug-only. MIE is an important production existence proof that MTE-class safety can move beyond debug-only deployments.

sections.title: Tag Confidentiality Enforcement (TCE)
sections.content: **Tag Confidentiality Enforcement (TCE)** is the response to TIKTAG: pointer-offset clamping (<4 GB), Spectre-v1 hardening, poisoning patterns (`0x2BAD`), VA layout tricks to prevent speculative tag leakage. TCE addresses the speculative tag-disclosure attack class that affected earlier MTE deployments.

sections.title: Status and deployment
sections.content:
Status (as of 2026-04): Apple announced production deployment on recent A-series devices with broad system coverage and an Enhanced Security path for third-party apps; exact device coverage, default-on scope, and third-party-app exposure are vendor- and release-specific and should be checked against current Apple platform documentation.

The combination of EMTE + secure typed allocators + TCE is currently the most ambitious production memory-safety stack on consumer silicon.
